title: EMU Discharge Summary - Left MTLE-HS
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Jane D. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1988-03-14 | Age: 37 | Sex: Female
Admission: 2026-01-06 | Discharge: 2026-01-10
Attending: Dr. Waters

REFERRAL
Referred by Dr. Liu (UNC Epilepsy) for Phase 1 EMU evaluation. Patient is right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Drug-resistant focal seizures since age 19. Admitted for Phase 1 evaluation to characterize seizures and assess surgical candidacy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. D. is a 37-year-old right-handed woman with an 18-year history of focal epilepsy. Seizures began at age 19 with no clear precipitant. She reports two seizure types: (1) frequent auras consisting of a rising epigastric sensation with deja vu, occurring multiple times per week; (2) focal seizures with impaired awareness approximately 2-3 times per month, characterized by behavioral arrest, oral automatisms, and left hand fumbling, lasting 60-90 seconds with post-ictal confusion for 5-10 minutes. Last convulsive seizure was over 2 years ago. She has failed four ASMs: carbamazepine (rash), valproic acid (weight gain, tremor), zonisamide (cognitive side effects), and phenytoin (inadequate control).

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1500 mg twice daily
2. Lamotrigine (Lamictal) 200 mg twice daily
No rescue medications currently prescribed.

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- Mild depression (on sertraline 50 mg)
- Anxiety disorder
- No history of TBI, stroke, or CNS infection
- No suicidal ideation history

SOCIAL HISTORY
Currently employed as a teacher. Drives with neurologist awareness (seizure-free from GTC >2 years). Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
Patient was admitted on 2026-01-06 and connected to continuous video-EEG. ASMs were held per protocol. Four typical seizures were captured over 4 days.

Interictal EEG: Frequent left anterior temporal sharp waves (F7/T3), occasionally with phase reversal at T3. No generalized discharges.

Ictal EEG: All four seizures showed left temporal rhythmic theta onset at T3, evolving to left temporal-parietal involvement. Semiology consistent with left mesial temporal onset: epigastric aura, then behavioral arrest with right hand automatisms and left hand dystonic posturing.

No events were concerning for FND.

MRI BRAIN (3T Epilepsy Protocol, 2025-11-20)
ABNORMAL. Left hippocampal sclerosis with T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and volume loss of the left hippocampus. No other lesions identified. Lateralization: Left. Localization: Mesial temporal.

FDG-PET (2025-12-15)
Left temporal hypometabolism concordant with MRI and EEG findings.

NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL TESTING
Modest verbal memory deficits consistent with left temporal dysfunction. Otherwise intact.

ASSESSMENT
1. Drug-resistant left mesial temporal lobe epilepsy (MTLE) with hippocampal sclerosis (MTLE-HS)
2. Concordant data: left temporal interictal and ictal EEG, left hippocampal sclerosis on MRI, left temporal hypometabolism on PET
3. Medically refractory epilepsy - failed 4 ASMs

PLAN
- Patient is a strong surgical candidate. Surgical options including left anterior temporal lobectomy (ATL) or selective amygdalohippocampectomy (SAH) were discussed in detail.
- Patient expressed interest in proceeding with surgical evaluation.
- Refer to Multidisciplinary Epilepsy Case Conference (MECC).
- fMRI for language lateralization ordered.
- WADA test may be needed depending on fMRI results.
- Continue levetiracetam and lamotrigine at current doses pending surgical planning.
- Follow-up in UNC Epilepsy clinic in 6 weeks.
- Patient is not currently driving per updated counseling (seizures captured during admission).

title: EMU Discharge Summary - FND/PNEE
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Marcus T. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1995-07-22 | Age: 30 | Sex: Male
Admission: 2026-01-13 | Discharge: 2026-01-18
Attending: Dr. Wabulya

REFERRAL
Referred by external neurology for characterization of events. Patient is right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Spells of unresponsiveness with shaking since age 27. Referred for EMU to differentiate epilepsy from non-epileptic events.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. T. is a 30-year-old right-handed man who began having episodes at age 27 following a motor vehicle accident (no head injury documented). Episodes occur 2-4 times per month and are described by witnesses as sudden unresponsiveness with bilateral shaking that is asynchronous and waxing-waning, lasting 3-10 minutes. He sometimes recalls events partially. No post-ictal confusion. Events are often preceded by emotional stress or sleep deprivation. He was started on levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily by outside neurology without improvement. One outside EEG was normal.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1000 mg twice daily (prescribed for presumed seizures)
2. Escitalopram 10 mg daily (anxiety/depression)

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- PTSD (following MVA)
- Depression
- Anxiety
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- No suicidal ideation

SOCIAL HISTORY
Unemployed since onset of episodes. Does not drive. Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
Patient was admitted on 2026-01-13. ASMs continued during admission. Three typical events were captured over 5 days.

Interictal EEG: Normal background. No epileptiform discharges. No focal slowing.

Ictal EEG: All three captured events showed no EEG correlate. During events, there was muscle artifact but the underlying rhythm remained normal. Ictal semiology: asynchronous bilateral limb movements, eyes closed throughout (resisted opening), back arching, rapid return to baseline without post-ictal period.

No electrographic seizures were recorded.

MRI BRAIN (2025-09-10)
NORMAL. No structural abnormality identified.

ASSESSMENT
1. Functional neurological disorder (FND) / Psychogenic non-epileptic events (PNEE) - all captured events were non-epileptic
2. Comorbid PTSD, depression, and anxiety
3. Levetiracetam is not indicated - to be tapered and discontinued

PLAN
- Diagnosis of FND/PNEE discussed sensitively with patient and family.
- Levetiracetam to be tapered off over 4 weeks.
- Referred to UNC FND Clinic for specialized treatment (CBT, physical therapy).
- Psychology and psychiatry referral placed.
- Patient is not a surgical candidate.
- Patient cleared to drive pending 6-month event-free period per state guidelines.
- Follow-up in UNC FND Clinic in 4 weeks.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - Drug-Resistant Focal Epilepsy, Prior VNS
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Sandra K. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1979-11-05 | Age: 46 | Sex: Female
Admission: 2026-01-28 | Discharge: 2026-02-03
Attending: Dr. Sheikh

REFERRAL
Referred by Dr. Wabulya (UNC Epilepsy) for Phase 2 evaluation planning. Left-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Continued drug-resistant seizures despite VNS therapy and multiple ASMs. Admitted for Phase 1 re-evaluation.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. K. is a 46-year-old left-handed woman with a 22-year history of drug-resistant focal epilepsy. Seizure onset at age 24 with no identified precipitant. She has failed six ASMs over her lifetime. VNS was implanted in 2018 with approximately 30% reduction in seizure frequency but no seizure freedom. She continues to have focal seizures with impaired awareness approximately weekly, with occasional secondary generalization (last GTC 4 months ago). Seizures characterized by head version to the right, right arm tonic posturing, and impaired awareness lasting 1-2 minutes.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Lacosamide (Vimpat) 200 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam (Onfi) 10 mg twice daily
3. Topiramate (Topamax) 100 mg twice daily - patient reports word-finding difficulties
VNS magnet for rescue.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Vagus nerve stimulator (VNS) implanted 2018, currently active

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- Depression (on bupropion)
- No other neurological comorbidities
- No suicidal ideation history

SOCIAL HISTORY
Disabled, not working. Does not drive. Husband is primary care partner. Left-handed.

EMU COURSE
VNS was turned off on admission day. ASMs continued. Six seizures captured over 6 days.

Interictal EEG: Right frontal sharp waves (F4), right anterior temporal slowing (F8/T4).

Ictal EEG: Seizure onset from right frontal region (F4) with rapid spread to right temporal. Semiology consistent with right frontal onset: hypermotor features at onset, then head version left, tonic right arm.

MRI BRAIN (3T Epilepsy Protocol, 2025-08-14)
ABNORMAL. Small area of cortical thickening and T2 signal abnormality in the right posterior frontal lobe (premotor cortex), suspicious for focal cortical dysplasia (FCD). Lateralization: Right. Localization: Frontal.

FDG-PET (2025-10-02)
Right frontal hypometabolism concordant with MRI findings.

ASSESSMENT
1. Drug-resistant right frontal lobe epilepsy, likely due to focal cortical dysplasia (FCD)
2. Medically refractory - failed 6 ASMs, VNS with partial response
3. Lesion concordant with ictal onset zone

PLAN
- Patient is a surgical candidate. Given right frontal FCD, LITT (laser interstitial thermal therapy) discussed as minimally invasive option.
- Presented at MECC - recommendation for Phase 2 evaluation with sEEG to confirm margins.
- Topiramate to be tapered due to cognitive side effects; cenobamate (Xcopri) to be trialed.
- VNS reactivated on discharge.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy in 6 weeks.
- Not driving.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - JME Medication Adjustment
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Derek M. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 2001-04-17 | Age: 24 | Sex: Male
Admission: 2026-02-10 | Discharge: 2026-02-14
Attending: Dr. LaRoche

REFERRAL
Referred by Dr. Bajpai (UNC Epilepsy) for medication cross-titration and seizure characterization. Right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Breakthrough generalized tonic-clonic seizures on current ASM regimen. Admitted for medication adjustment and characterization of myoclonic jerks.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. M. is a 24-year-old right-handed man with a history of juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME) diagnosed at age 17. He initially responded well to valproic acid but it was discontinued 2 years ago due to significant weight gain and tremor. He was transitioned to levetiracetam which has provided partial control. He continues to have morning myoclonic jerks daily, primarily upon awakening, and has had 3 generalized tonic-clonic seizures in the past 6 months, all associated with sleep deprivation. He denies absence spells.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levetiracetam (Keppra) 2000 mg twice daily
No rescue medications.

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- No neurological comorbidities
- Mild anxiety
- No depression, no suicidal ideation

SOCIAL HISTORY
College student. Does not currently drive due to recent GTC seizures. Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
ASMs continued. Sleep deprivation protocol employed. Two events captured - both myoclonic jerks in the morning after sleep deprivation, and one GTC on day 3.

Interictal EEG: Generalized 3-4 Hz polyspike-and-wave and spike-and-wave discharges, maximum frontally, activated by sleep deprivation. No focal features.

Ictal EEG: GTC preceded by a burst of generalized polyspike-and-wave discharges. Myoclonic jerks correlated with brief generalized polyspike bursts.

MRI BRAIN (2024-06-01)
NORMAL. No structural abnormality.

ASSESSMENT
1. Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME) - confirmed by EEG and clinical features
2. Breakthrough seizures likely related to medication switch from valproic acid and ongoing sleep deprivation
3. Levetiracetam providing incomplete control

PLAN
- Add lamotrigine (Lamictal) with slow titration to 100 mg twice daily (cross-titration while continuing levetiracetam).
- Discussed importance of sleep hygiene and alcohol avoidance.
- Patient is NOT a surgical candidate (idiopathic generalized epilepsy).
- Driving restriction maintained until seizure-free for 6 months.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy in 8 weeks for medication check.
- Seizure action plan provided. No rescue medication prescribed at this time.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - Post-Stroke Epilepsy
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Howard L. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1958-09-30 | Age: 67 | Sex: Male
Admission: 2026-02-17 | Discharge: 2026-02-22
Attending: Dr. Shin

REFERRAL
Referred by UNC Neurology (non-epilepsy) following new-onset seizures in setting of prior stroke. Right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
New-onset focal seizures 8 months after right MCA ischemic stroke. Admitted to characterize seizure type and optimize medication.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. L. is a 67-year-old right-handed man who suffered a right middle cerebral artery (MCA) ischemic stroke in May 2025 with residual mild left hemiparesis. He developed focal seizures approximately 8 months post-stroke, occurring 2-3 times per month. Seizures are described as focal motor events involving left face and arm twitching with retained awareness, lasting 30-60 seconds. No secondary generalization documented. He was started on levetiracetam by the stroke team with partial improvement but seizures continue.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1000 mg twice daily
2. Aspirin 81 mg daily
3. Atorvastatin 40 mg daily
4. Lisinopril 10 mg daily
No rescue medications.

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- Right MCA ischemic stroke (May 2025) with residual mild left hemiparesis
- Hypertension
- Hyperlipidemia
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- No psychiatric comorbidities
- No suicidal ideation

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired. Wife is primary care partner. Does not drive since stroke. Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
ASM dose reduced per protocol. Three focal motor seizures captured.

Interictal EEG: Right hemispheric slowing (intermittent rhythmic delta) maximal over right temporal-parietal region. No independent left hemisphere abnormalities. Occasional right temporal sharp waves.

Ictal EEG: Seizures originated from right centroparietal region (C4/P4) with focal rhythmic beta then theta activity. Semiology: contralateral (left) face and arm clonic movements.

MRI BRAIN (2026-01-08)
ABNORMAL. Encephalomalacia and gliosis in the right MCA territory involving right temporal and parietal lobes, consistent with prior ischemic stroke. Lateralization: Right. Localization: Temporal and parietal.

ASSESSMENT
1. Post-stroke focal epilepsy, right hemisphere, likely from stroke encephalomalacia
2. Seizures are focal motor with retained awareness (Jackson march pattern)
3. Medically refractory - on one ASM with persistent seizures

PLAN
- Increase levetiracetam to 1500 mg twice daily.
- Add lacosamide (Vimpat) 100 mg twice daily with titration to 150 mg twice daily.
- Patient is not currently a surgical candidate given eloquent cortex involvement and medical comorbidities; may be reconsidered in future if medications fail.
- RNS (responsive neurostimulator) discussed as potential future option.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy in 6 weeks.
- Not driving.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - Mixed FND and Epilepsy
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Priya N. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1990-12-01 | Age: 35 | Sex: Female
Admission: 2026-03-01 | Discharge: 2026-03-05
Attending: Dr. Vaughn

REFERRAL
Referred by Dr. Sheikh (UNC Epilepsy) for characterization. Right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Two distinct event types - one suspected epileptic, one suspected non-epileptic. Admitted for video-EEG characterization.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. N. is a 35-year-old right-handed woman with two distinct event types since age 28. Event type 1: brief right-hand tingling with visual changes lasting 10-20 seconds, occurring 1-2 times per week - suspected focal aware seizures. Event type 2: prolonged unresponsiveness with bilateral shaking lasting 5-15 minutes, occurring monthly, often after emotional stress - suspected non-epileptic events. She was started on oxcarbazepine with modest improvement in event type 1 frequency only. Prior history of sexual abuse, PTSD, and depression.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Oxcarbazepine (Trileptal) 300 mg twice daily
2. Sertraline 100 mg daily
3. Clonazepam 0.5 mg as needed for anxiety
No standing rescue ASM.

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- PTSD
- Depression
- Anxiety
- History of suicidal ideation (remote, no recent)
- No neurological comorbidities
- No prior epilepsy surgery

SOCIAL HISTORY
Works part-time. Does not drive. Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
Two event type 1 events and one event type 2 captured.

Interictal EEG: Left occipital sharp waves (O1). No generalized discharges.

Ictal EEG - Event type 1: Left occipital rhythmic alpha activity with evolution, consistent with focal aware seizure of left occipital onset. Semiology: right visual field flickering, then right hand paresthesias.

Ictal EEG - Event type 2: No EEG correlate. Prolonged muscle artifact with preserved background. Semiology consistent with PNEE: waxing-waning bilateral asynchronous movements, eyes closed, rapid recovery.

MRI BRAIN (2025-06-20)
ABNORMAL. Small area of T2 signal change in left occipital lobe, possibly representing a small cortical dysplasia vs gliosis. Lateralization: Left. Localization: Occipital.

ASSESSMENT
1. Mixed disorder: focal aware epilepsy (left occipital onset) AND functional neurological disorder (PNEE)
2. Comorbid PTSD and depression likely contributing to FND component

PLAN
- Continue oxcarbazepine for epileptic component.
- Refer to UNC FND Clinic for non-epileptic event management (CBT, physical therapy).
- Psychiatry follow-up for PTSD and depression management.
- Not a surgical candidate at this time given mixed disorder and small/uncertain lesion.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy in 6 weeks.
- Not driving.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - Autoimmune Epilepsy
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Calvin R. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 1972-06-14 | Age: 53 | Sex: Male
Admission: 2026-03-10 | Discharge: 2026-03-15
Attending: Dr. Liu

REFERRAL
Referred by UNC Non-Neurology (Rheumatology) for new-onset seizures with suspected autoimmune etiology. Right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
New-onset seizures with subacute cognitive decline over 4 months. Admitted for characterization and workup.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. R. is a 53-year-old right-handed man who presented 4 months ago with subacute onset of confusion, short-term memory loss, and new-onset seizures. Seizures are described as focal with impaired awareness - he stares, makes chewing movements, and is unresponsive for 1-2 minutes, occurring daily. No prior seizure history. CSF showed elevated protein and mild pleocytosis. LGI1 antibody returned positive in serum and CSF. He was started on methylprednisolone with partial improvement in cognition but seizures persist.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1500 mg twice daily
2. Lacosamide (Vimpat) 100 mg twice daily
3. Prednisone 40 mg daily (tapering)
No rescue medications.

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis (newly diagnosed)
- Mild cognitive impairment (secondary to encephalitis)
- Hypertension
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- No psychiatric comorbidities
- No suicidal ideation

SOCIAL HISTORY
Accountant, currently on medical leave. Wife is primary care partner. Does not drive. Right-handed.

EMU COURSE
ASMs continued. Multiple seizures captured daily.

Interictal EEG: Bilateral temporal slowing with left greater than right. Left temporal independent sharp waves. Diffuse background slowing consistent with encephalopathy.

Ictal EEG: Seizures originated from left temporal region with rhythmic theta onset at T3, spreading to bilateral temporal regions. Semiology: focal impaired awareness with orofacial automatisms.

MRI BRAIN (2026-02-28)
ABNORMAL. T2/FLAIR hyperintensity involving bilateral hippocampi and amygdalae, left greater than right, consistent with limbic encephalitis. Lateralization: Bilateral. Localization: Mesial temporal bilaterally.

ASSESSMENT
1. LGI1 autoimmune limbic encephalitis with focal epilepsy
2. Autoimmune/inflammatory etiology
3. Bilateral mesial temporal involvement - not a surgical candidate
4. Seizures ongoing despite 2 ASMs - consider escalating immunotherapy

PLAN
- Not a surgical candidate given autoimmune etiology and bilateral involvement.
- IVIG course to be initiated (5-day course).
- Rituximab discussed as maintenance immunotherapy.
- Neurology (epilepsy and autoimmune) co-management.
- Continue levetiracetam and lacosamide.
- Driving restricted.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy and Autoimmune Neurology in 4 weeks.

title: EMU Discharge Summary - Pediatric-Onset Epilepsy, Dravet Syndrome
note_text_clean:
EPILEPSY MONITORING UNIT DISCHARGE SUMMARY

Patient: Leo W. (synthetic/de-identified)
DOB: 2008-02-09 | Age: 18 | Sex: Male
Admission: 2026-03-24 | Discharge: 2026-03-28
Attending: Dr. Wabulya

REFERRAL
Transition from pediatric to adult epilepsy care. Referred by pediatric neurology for transition and re-evaluation. Right-handed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR ADMISSION
Transition evaluation for known Dravet syndrome. Admitted for medication optimization and adult care transition.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. W. is an 18-year-old right-handed male with Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation confirmed) diagnosed at age 1. He had febrile status epilepticus as an infant and has had drug-resistant epilepsy throughout childhood. Multiple seizure types include: (1) generalized tonic-clonic seizures - currently occurring monthly; (2) focal seizures with impaired awareness - weekly; (3) myoclonic jerks - daily. He has significant intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder. He has failed numerous medications. Currently has intranasal diazepam as rescue medication.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Valproic acid (Depakote) 750 mg twice daily
2. Clobazam (Onfi) 10 mg twice daily
3. Fenfluramine (Fintepla) 0.2 mg/kg/day
Rescue: Intranasal diazepam (Valtoco) as needed

PAST MEDICAL / NEUROLOGICAL HISTORY
- Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation)
- Intellectual disability (moderate)
- Autism spectrum disorder
- No prior epilepsy surgery
- No psychiatric comorbidities beyond ASD
- No suicidal ideation (not applicable given cognitive level)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Lives with parents. Mother is primary care partner. Does not drive (cognitive/seizure). Right-handed. Attends adult day program.

EMU COURSE
Continuation of home medications. Seizures captured: one GTC on day 2, multiple myoclonic events.

Interictal EEG: Generalized irregular spike-and-wave and polyspike-and-wave discharges. Background slowing. Occasional right temporal focal slowing.

Ictal EEG: GTC with generalized polyspike then tonic-clonic activity. Myoclonic jerks with brief generalized polyspike bursts.

MRI BRAIN (2024-11-15)
NORMAL for age. No hippocampal sclerosis or cortical dysplasia.

ASSESSMENT
1. Dravet syndrome (SCN1A) - confirmed genetic epilepsy syndrome
2. Drug-resistant generalized and focal epilepsy
3. Successful transition to adult epilepsy care
4. Not a surgical candidate (genetic/generalized epilepsy)

PLAN
- Continue current regimen: valproic acid, clobazam, fenfluramine.
- Discuss adding cannabidiol (Epidiolex) given FDA approval for Dravet syndrome.
- Not a surgical candidate.
- Ensure rescue medication supply (intranasal diazepam) - refill provided.
- Care coordination with adult day program and primary care.
- Follow-up UNC Epilepsy (adult) in 3 months.
- Cardiac monitoring for fenfluramine continued (annual echo scheduled).
